inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
You want to rebuild conservatively after a messy few months and be healthy enough to race a half marathon this spring — that’s a clear, sensible target and the right priority is avoiding setbacks.
Before I write a safe, conservative return plan I only need two quick details from you:
1) Time availability — how many days per week can you realistically commit to training, and how much time can you give per session (example: 3 days, 45–60 minutes)?
2) Injury status — any current pain, recent injuries or surgeries, or medical clearance concerns? If you’re coming back from an injury, say what stage you’re at (e.g., fully pain-free, doing rehab work, still under treatment).
Reply with those two items and I’ll build a conservative, easy-to-follow rebuild plan tailored to your schedule and risk profile.
Coach

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
You’ve given the pieces I needed: target half on 2026-05-17, 4 sessions/week and ~4–5 hours available, mild Achilles tightness, and busy Tuesday mornings — I’ll keep this week deliberately conservative and simple so you protect consistency.
Plan for the week (minimum effective dose, four sessions):
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic (60–75 min): conversational pace, flat route, steady effort. Warm up 10 minutes easy; keep cadence light and posture tall. Skip any pickups if the Achilles feels tight.
- Session 2 — Mobility (20–30 min, schedule around your busy Tuesday morning): short session you can do later in the day — calf mobility, 1–2 gentle calf stretches, ankle circles, 5–8 minutes single-leg balance/activation, and hip-openers. Finish with brief breathing/downshift work.
- Session 3 — Easy aerobic (45–60 min): another easy steady run mid-to-late week. Keep it relaxed — your aerobic anchor for the week. Avoid hills and fast efforts.
- Session 4 — Strength (30–40 min, weekend): low-load strength and calf control: glute bridges, single-leg RDLs, 2–3 sets of controlled calf lowers (slow 6–8 reps, pain-free range), planks or anti-rotation core. Keep loads modest — focus on control rather than heaviness.
Estimated time: this core plan is ~3–3.5 hours. If you want to use the full 4–5 hours some weeks, add one of these optional extras: extend one easy run by 10–20 minutes or add a 30–45 minute very-easy recovery jog or long walk on an easy day.
Key priorities and safety rules (read before every session):
- Energy is the limiting signal: if you feel notably low, shorten or skip the session rather than pushing through. If energy rebounds the next day, resume with caution — don’t try to “make up” hard work.
- Protect the Achilles: thorough warm-up, avoid hills and sudden speed, stop for sharp pain. If symptoms worsen or you get a new catching/sharp pain, pause running and get it checked.
- Keep cadence light, posture tall, and focus on sleep, nutrition, and downshifting stress — those matter more than minutes this week.
- No hard sessions while risk is elevated. This week is intentionally low intensity.
That’s the week — clear, structured, and conservative so you build back consistency without flaring the Achilles. Let me know how the first two sessions feel and I’ll adjust next week.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Availability update noted: you have four sessions this week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday, Sunday), totaling roughly 4–5 hours. Pain is 2/10 and the risk assessment remains green. We’ll keep the load conservative and follow the updated base-plan focused on rebuilding fitness safely for your half marathon in May.
Weekly plan (four sessions):
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic: 60–75 minutes, flat loop, conversational pace. Prioritize smooth form, no hills, and easy breathing. Keep cadence light and posture tall. If pain or fatigue develops, downgrade to easy effort and stop if sharp or increasing pain.
- Session 2 — Mobility / prehab: 20–30 minutes. Schedule around your Tuesday mornings if possible; if needed, shift to later in the week. Gentle mobility and soft tissue work to support the Achilles.
- Session 3 — Easy aerobic: 45–60 minutes, mid-to-late week. Maintain relaxed effort and avoid speed work.
- Session 4 — Strength: 30–40 minutes, weekend. Low-load, calf control, and stability work to support running form and Achilles health.
Key guidance and safety:
- Thorough warm-up before any run; avoid hills and abrupt speed increases while Achilles is sensitive.
- Keep the pace easy; focus on form, not tempo or thresholds.
- Sleep and simple recovery habits matter—prioritize consistency.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and don’t chase missed intensity later in the week.
- No hard sessions if the risk is higher than green.
Updates and quick checks:
- Report back after the first two sessions on how they felt.
- If work shifts affect timing, you can swap a session by a few hours within the week.
If you want, I can adjust numbers slightly once you’ve tried the first two sessions, but we’ll keep it simple and safe for now. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Your 45-minute easy run yesterday went well, with a pain score of 2/10 and risk still green. We’ll keep load light this week while you rebuild fitness for the May 17 half marathon.
Plan for the week (Mon, Wed, Fri, Sun):
- Session 1 — Monday: Easy Aerobic 60–75 minutes
- Flat route, conversational pace
- Focus on a relaxed cadence and tall posture; avoid hills or hard surges
- Thorough warm-up (5–10 min easy jog + gentle dynamic mobility) and a calm cooldown
- After run: light mobility work if you feel tight
- Session 2 — Wednesday: Mobility / Prehab 20–30 minutes
- Gentle, non-fatiguing moves (ankle circles, calf/soleus mobility, hip opening, light glute activation)
- Can be completed in one block or split into two shorter blocks if needed
- Schedule around a busy morning; avoid Tuesday morning as discussed
- Session 3 — Friday: Easy Aerobic 45–60 minutes
- Similar approach to Session 1: flat course, conversational pace
- Prioritize warm-up, low load, and a short cooldown
- Session 4 — Sunday: Strength 30–40 minutes
- Low-load, calf control and stability focus with simple resistance
- Example moves (2 sets of 8–12 reps each): bodyweight squats, glute bridges, calf raises, and a light single-leg balance drill
- Keep the intensity low and progress only if you feel good
Key safety notes
- Maintain thorough warm-ups, stay off hills, and avoid any sharp increases in intensity
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week
General reminders
- Sleep, hydration, and steady recovery habits support this rebuild
- You’re building a solid base for the May race without overloading the Achilles
Let me know how the first two sessions feel once you’ve completed them.

inbound.subject: Long run felt fine
inbound.body: Long run update: I got through 75 minutes this morning and honestly I feel ok after the long run. The Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and I slept well last night. I'd like to keep the same four-day rhythm this week if that still makes sense.

outbound.subject: Re: Long run felt fine
outbound.text:
75 minutes today went smoothly—the Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and you slept well. Let’s keep your four-day rhythm this week but stay conservative to protect the tendon.
Plan for the week:
- Monday: Easy aerobic, 60–75 minutes on flat ground. Do a thorough warm-up, keep the pace conversational, and finish with a calm cooldown. Cadence light, posture tall.
- Wednesday: Mobility and prehab, 20–30 minutes (ankle/calf/hip work) with light activation. If Tuesday morning is tricky, you can split into two shorter blocks.
- Friday: Easy aerobic, 45–60 minutes, flat route, easy pace, with a strong warm-up and cooldown.
- Sunday: Strength session, 30–40 minutes, low-load calf control and stability work. Maintain control and avoid heavy loading.
Safety and pacing:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and don’t try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Keep load conservative: no hills, no hard efforts, and stay focused on warm-ups and recovery.
You’re doing the right things by listening to your body. Let me know if the Achilles status changes, otherwise go ahead with the plan.

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
Your comeback runs should feel truly easy and controlled today, given that your Achilles is quiet in the background. Here’s how to gauge easy:
- Aim for an RPE of about 2–4/10, with relaxed breathing and shoulders, and an easy, smooth cadence. You should be able to carry on a short conversation during the run.
- The pace should be noticeably slower than race or harder-session pace. Don’t try to chase speed on these easy days; you’re building the base and protecting the tendon.
- If you start to feel any Achilles tightness, or sharp pain, drop the effort to an even easier level or shorten the run. Keep the warm-up thorough and avoid hills or any sudden speed changes.
- Today you have the option to do a short mobility or recovery session instead of running. If you choose to move, keep it light and focused on ankles, calves, and hips.
- Maintain your four-day rhythm this week and stay conservative to protect the tendon.
If you want a quick check after your first two sessions, share how the effort, pain, and recovery felt and we’ll adjust as needed.

inbound.subject: Work got messy
inbound.body: Can we adjust the week? Work is crazy and I probably only have Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday for training. I missed one session already and my stress is definitely up.

outbound.subject: Re: Work got messy
outbound.text:
Your week is tight and stress is clearly up. We’ll keep things ultra-conservative and fit the essential work on Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday to protect the Achilles and keep your momentum.
Plan for this week
- Wednesday — easy_aerobic 30–40 minutes on flat ground. Start with a thorough warm-up, finish with a calm cooldown. Keep effort light (RPE around 2–3/10), relaxed breathing, and tidy mechanics. If Achilles feels tight, add 5–10 minutes of mobility or swap to a very short easy jog/walk combo.
- Friday — strength session 20–30 minutes, focused on low-load calf control, ankle stability, and hip activation. Stay light, control tempo, and avoid high impact. No missed reps if form drifts; keep it smooth and relaxed.
- Sunday — easy_aerobic 40–60 minutes on flat ground. Maintain a conversational pace, no hills, and a steady cadence. Finish with a brief mobility/mobility-only option if you feel tired or if Achilles tightness flares up.
Guidance and safety this week
- If energy drops further, shorten any session rather than forcing it to full length. If you rebound the next day, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” lost work.
- Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and overall stress management to support recovery and tendon health. Warm-ups stay thorough; avoid hills and sudden intensity changes.
- If Achilles pain increases or sharp pain appears, stop and reassess. Consider a quick mobility routine or prehab instead of running.
You’re rebuilding with a minimum effective dose, and that’s the plan this week. Let me know how each session feels and we’ll adjust next week as needed. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Here are the details you probably need
inbound.body: More detail for the adjustment: event date is 2026-05-17, days available this week are 3, pain score is still 2 out of 10, risk candidate feels yellow because work stress is high, week is chaotic true, stress score 8, sleep score 5.

outbound.subject: Re: Here are the details you probably need
outbound.text:
You’ve got the race date (2026-05-17), three training days this week (Wed/Fri/Sun), pain 2/10, high work stress (8/10) and poor sleep (5/10) — so we keep the ultra-conservative, 3-session plan this week to protect the Achilles and maintain momentum.
Plan (do these exactly as written):
Wed — Easy aerobic (30–40 min)
- Flat, conversational pace, RPE ~2–3/10. Warm up 8–10 minutes easy (walk→jog) and finish with a calm 5–8 minute cooldown and gentle calf mobility. 
- If Achilles feels tight, add 5–10 min of mobility and shorten the run rather than pushing through discomfort.
Fri — Strength (20–30 min, low load, control-focused)
- Keep it light and slow; focus on control, balance, and hip stability. Example: 3x10 double-leg heel raises (slow 3s up/3s down), 2x8 single-leg balance holds (30s each), 2x12 glute bridges, 2x30s banded side-steps. 
- Avoid heavy or aggressive eccentric loading this week; stop any move that causes sharp Achilles pain.
Sun — Easy aerobic (40–60 min)
- Conversational pace on flat ground. RPE ~2–3/10. If energy or sleep are worse than expected, shorten to 25–35 min — preserve quality, not quantity.
Execution priorities (this week only):
- Keep effort smooth, relaxed, and technically tidy. No hills or pickups. 
- Monitor pain and fatigue closely: if pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to an easy walk/jog or skip. Do NOT try to make up missed intensity later in the week. 
- If pain becomes sharp, swelling appears, or pain increases by ~2 points, stop and seek medical review.
Recovery & stress control (practical steps):
- Aim for one consistent block of sleep and a short daytime reset where possible (20–30 min nap or a 10-minute breathing break). 
- Prioritize one solid evening meal and hydration around sessions. 
- Short breathing or mobility breaks during work can reduce stress load and help sleep.
That’s the plan for the week — minimal, controlled, and race-focused. Tell me after Sunday’s session how it felt (perceived effort, Achilles response, and sleep) and we’ll adjust next week based on that feedback.

inbound.subject: Still on the same four-day pattern
inbound.body: Just to confirm, I'm still doing four days per week most weeks. The same general Monday Wednesday Friday Sunday pattern still works, although Friday has become the easiest day for me to protect.

outbound.subject: Re: Still on the same four-day pattern
outbound.text:
You're still on a four-day pattern (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun), and Friday remains your easier-to-protect day. With your Achilles status and the goal to rebuild gradually, this week stays conservative and sticks to the minimum effective dose while keeping you moving.
Updated plan for this week:
- Session 1 — Monday: easy_aerobic, 30–40 minutes on flat ground. 8–10 minute warm-up, 5–8 minute cooldown. Optional 5–10 minutes mobility if energy allows. Keep effort easy (RPE 2–3/10). No hills. Thorough warm-up for the Achilles. Stop if pain increases.
- Session 2 — Wednesday: strength, 20–30 minutes, low-load, control-focused. Include light heel raises, balance holds, glute bridges, banded side-steps. Keep movements smooth; avoid heavy eccentric work.
- Session 3 — Friday: easy_aerobic, 20–30 minutes on flat ground. Keep it light (RPE 2–3). If energy is very low, shorten the duration.
- Session 4 — Sunday: skills, 20–30 minutes. Technique and form-focused drills (cadence, knee drive, arm swing), plus light mobility. Keep intensity very low and avoid hills or high-impact work.
Key guidance:
- Energy is the limiting signal: stay conservative today and this week.
- Protect consistency with the minimum effective dose.
- If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing it.
- If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to make up lost work.
- Achilles safety: thorough warm-up, avoid hills, avoid heavy eccentric work, monitor pain. Stop and seek evaluation if pain sharpens or worsens by 2 points.
Optional: If you want to adjust start times for work, I can help—but the plan structure stays the same.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Travel week coming up
inbound.body: Travel week heads-up: next week is rough. I only have Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday open, and hotel treadmill plus a tiny gym are all I'll have. I'd love the simplest travel-friendly version of the plan.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel week coming up
outbound.text:
Travel week is here and you have only Tue, Thu, and Sat with a hotel treadmill and a tiny gym, so we will keep it ultra-simple and conservative to protect your Achilles and maintain momentum.
Three travel-friendly sessions:
- Tuesday — Easy Aerobic: 25–35 minutes on a flat treadmill. 10 min warm-up, 15–20 min steady easy running, 5–10 min cool-down. Keep effort at RPE 2–3/10. No hills or fast pickups. If energy is low, cut to 20 minutes total. End with light mobility if you have energy.
- Thursday — Strength: 20–30 minutes, low-load and technique-focused. Use bodyweight or light resistance. Examples: glute bridges, bodyweight squats, hip hinges, planks. 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps per exercise; rest 30–60 seconds. Keep it controlled; avoid heavy eccentric work.
- Saturday — Skills/Technique: 20–30 minutes. Cadence checks, arm swing tidy, light running drills. Keep it smooth and relaxed; finish with gentle mobility.
Notes:
- Energy is the limiting factor. Stay conservative and, if needed, shorten rather than pushing through.
- If Achilles pain sharpens or worsens by about 2 points, stop and reassess.
- Stay on flat surfaces, avoid hills, avoid heavy eccentric work. Thorough warm-up, and keep reps and loads light.
- Sleep, nutrition, and downshifting stress matter—protect them.
We will resume the normal four-day rhythm again after you are back and able to rejoin the full base plan. If you are feeling good after the week, you will return with some momentum.

Tuesday — Easy aerobic on a flat hotel treadmill, 25–35 minutes total. 10 min warm-up, 15–20 min easy running (RPE 2–3), 5–10 min cool-down. If energy is lower, cut to 20 minutes.

inbound.subject: Hotel treadmill check-in
inbound.body: Travel check-in: I did a run for 40m on the hotel treadmill, about 4.5 miles, avg hr 146, and roughly 520 calories. Felt decent, slept ok, and nothing got worse. Mostly just mentally flat from travel.

outbound.subject: Re: Hotel treadmill check-in
outbound.text:
Your travel week update is on the board: the 40-minute hotel treadmill run (about 4.5 miles) with an average HR around 146, Achilles pain about 2/10, and you slept okay. That’s a solid step, and we’ll keep the week conservative and anchored.
Two anchor sessions this travel week (keep them easy) and one optional skill/tech session if energy allows:
- Tue — Easy Aerobic Anchor: 25–35 minutes on a flat treadmill. 10 min warm-up, 15–20 min easy running, 5–10 min cooldown. Target RPE 2–3. If energy is very low, shorten to 20 minutes.
- Thu — Low-Load Strength Anchor: 20–30 minutes. Bodyweight or light resistance; 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps. Avoid heavy eccentrics; include mobility.
- Sat — Skills/Technique (optional): 20–30 minutes. Cadence checks, arm swing, light drills; finish with gentle mobility.
All sessions stay easy, with a tall posture and light cadence. Recovery should be simple: prioritize sleep, hydration, and low-friction routines.
Safety note: monitor Achilles closely. Stop immediately if pain sharpens or worsens by about 2 points. After each session, note perceived effort, Achilles tightness/pain, and sleep/recovery. If anything changes, adjust the plan accordingly and keep the anchors intact.
If you’re feeling good, you can run the Tue session as a touch longer within the 25–35 minute window, but never at the expense of form or comfort. Let’s keep things steady and consolidate this week toward your race goal on 2026-05-17.

inbound.subject: Back home and Saturday is open
inbound.body: Good news: for the next month Saturday is open again, so I have more flexibility for the long run. Week to week I still want to stay at four days, but now the long run doesn't have to be Sunday every time.

outbound.subject: Re: Back home and Saturday is open
outbound.text:
Saturday long run moved to Saturday, opening a clear window for that key session while keeping four days per week. Here’s the updated week, with the long run on Saturday and the other three days keeping to the conservative, injury-conscious plan you’re following.
Monday — easy_aerobic: 30–35 minutes on flat ground. 8–10 min warm-up, 5–8 min cooldown; optional mobility. Target RPE 2–3.
Wednesday — strength: 20–25 minutes of low-load work (bodyweight or light resistance). 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps, with mobility included. Keep it controlled and smooth.
Friday — easy_aerobic: 20–25 minutes on flat ground. 5–10 min warm-up, 5–10 min cooldown; RPE 2–3.
Saturday — long run: 40–45 minutes easy on flat ground. Focus on a relaxed, steady pace; maintain good form. Include a 5–10 min warm-up and cooldown; RPE 2–3.
Key reminders:
- Energy is the limiting signal, so today stays conservative. Keep effort smooth, relaxed, and technically tidy.
- Protect consistency with the minimum effective dose. Sleep, food, and downshifting overall stress matter.
- Achilles tightness remains low (around 2/10). Thorough warm-ups, avoiding hills and sudden intensity, and stopping if pain sharpens or worsens by ~2 points are essential.
- After each session, report perceived effort, Achilles tightness/pain, and sleep/recovery.
If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than trying to finish. If you rebound tomorrow, resume with caution instead of making up missed work.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Family chaos week
inbound.body: This week got blown up by family stuff. I only got in one 30m run and I'm feeling low energy today. Can we just simplify everything for a few days and keep the important things important?

outbound.subject: Re: Family chaos week
outbound.text:
You only managed one 30‑minute run this week and you’re feeling low energy — we’ll simplify the next few days and protect the two anchor sessions so the week still counts.
Plan for the next few days (simple, minimum effective dose):
- Today / next 48 hours: optional short mobility or recovery only (10–15 minutes of easy mobility, walking, foam rolling). Prioritize sleep, hydration, and low‑friction routines.
- Midweek touch (keep flexible): a short strength/mobility session — 20 minutes of low‑load, control‑focused work (bodyweight or light resistance, 2–3 sets of 8–12). If energy is very low, drop to a 10–12 minute mobility sequence instead.
- Friday (anchor — protect this): easiest aerobic 20–25 minutes on flat ground, RPE ~2–3. Short warm‑up (5–8 min) and 5 min cooldown. If you feel wiped, 15 minutes easy is fine — protect the quality, not duration.
- Saturday (anchor — long run): 40–45 minutes easy on flat (5–10 min warm‑up and 5 min cooldown), RPE ~2–3. If that’s not doable, do an easier 25–30 minute continuous run/walk — continuity beats forced volume this week.
Execution and safety notes:
- Keep cadence light, posture tall, avoid hills or any pickups. No hard intensity while risk is green. Thorough warm‑ups before any running.
- Achilles: if pain becomes sharp or increases by ~2 points on a 0–10 scale, stop and seek evaluation. Otherwise keep loads low and controlled.
- If the week gets tighter, drop everything except the two anchors — any extra session should stay easy and optional.
Short and practical recovery priorities: sleep, hydration, and predictable low‑friction routines (nutrition, easy evenings). 
Tell me how the Saturday run (or the anchors) feel and report perceived effort plus Achilles tightness (0–10).

inbound.subject: Details for the family-chaos week
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 2. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Family stuff blew up this week. I only got in a 30m run. Felt low energy, sore, poor sleep. Stress score is 9. I am worried about losing momentum.

outbound.subject: Re: Details for the family-chaos week
outbound.text:
You reported high stress (9/10), poor sleep, low energy and Achilles at 3/10 — we’ll keep this week deliberately minimal and protective and only do the two easy anchor sessions you have available.
Today / next 48 hours
- Keep activity very easy and short. Optional 10–15 minutes of gentle mobility/active recovery if you have the energy (ankle/calf mobility, hip swings, easy walking). No strengthening or hard work today — monitor the Achilles closely.
Friday (anchor — easiest aerobic)
- Goal: 15–25 minutes very easy on flat ground. Include 5–8 minutes easy warm-up (walk/jog), main easy jog at RPE ~2–3, 3–5 minute cooldown.
- Focus: relaxed posture, light cadence, avoid hills and any pickups. If energy is very low, 15 minutes is fine.
Saturday (anchor — long/easy)
- Goal: 25–45 minutes easy. Target 40–45 if you feel OK; otherwise do a 25–30 minute run/walk. Same warm-up/cooldown and keep RPE ~2–3.
- No intensity, no hills, and avoid extra load. Prioritize completing it comfortably rather than distance or pace.
Safety & execution rules (must-follow)
- If Achilles pain increases by ~2 points or becomes sharp, stop immediately and seek evaluation.
- If pain or overall fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session (shorter or walk/run) or skip — do not try to make up intensity later in the week.
Recovery priorities
- Protect sleep first (even short naps help). Keep hydration and a simple protein-rich snack after sessions. Gentle mobility or icing after runs only if it helps soreness.
What I want from you
- After each anchor session (Friday and Saturday) send a very short note with: perceived effort, Achilles pain (0–10), and one-line sleep/recovery status. Report immediately if pain sharpens.
This keeps progress conservative but consistent toward the May 17 half while protecting the Achilles and your energy. Let me know how Friday and Saturday go.

inbound.subject: Back on track
inbound.body: Better week. I ran three times: 35m easy, 50m steady, and 80m today for about 9 miles. Felt good, slept well, and the Achilles feels quiet again. Same four-day rhythm still seems like the sweet spot.

outbound.subject: Re: Back on track
outbound.text:
Good to hear the Achilles feels quiet again after your three runs, and you’re sticking with the four-day rhythm. That consistency matters, especially with the week you’ve had.
Here’s how to lock in this week:
Today: optional short mobility or recovery only if you have energy.
Anchors (protect these two sessions):
- Friday: very easy on flat, 15–25 minutes total. 5–8 minute warm-up, main block at RPE 2–3, 3–5 minute cooldown. No hills; keep posture tall.
- Saturday: easy on flat, 25–45 minutes (40–45 if you feel good; otherwise 25–30 with run/walk). 5–8 minute warm-up, easy effort throughout, 3–5 minute cooldown.
Other days (if you have the energy): keep everything very easy and optional. Prioritize sleep, hydration, and simple recovery routines.
Cadence and safety
- Maintain a light cadence and a tall posture. Avoid hills and any intensity.
- If Achilles pain increases by about 2 points or becomes sharp, stop and seek evaluation.
Reporting (optional but helpful)
- After each anchor session (Friday and Saturday), note your perceived effort, Achilles pain (0–10), and a one-line sleep/recovery note.
Let me know how the Friday and Saturday anchors go.

inbound.subject: Should I add strides?
inbound.body: Question for you: since things are stable again, should I add strides after one easy run, or keep everything fully aerobic for now?

outbound.subject: Re: Should I add strides?
outbound.text:
You're asking whether to add strides after an easy run now that the Achilles feels quiet. Given the current stability and your preference to stay conservative, you can test a small strides progression in a controlled way instead of changing the whole week.
Try this after one easy run this week: 4 x 15-second strides on flat, with full recovery between reps (about 60–90 seconds of easy jog or walk). Keep the rest of that run entirely aerobic, with no hills or extra pickups. If anything hurts, stop and revert to easy running only.
If you tolerate that small test well, you may repeat a similar tiny strides block after a future easy run, but don’t increase volume or intensity yet. Continue to protect your four-day rhythm and keep Friday and Saturday as easy, flat sessions.
Let me know how the test goes after you try it, and we’ll adjust based on how the Achilles responds.

inbound.subject: Harder session left me cooked
inbound.body: Yesterday I did a harder session: total run 55m with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Today I'm pretty cooked, sleep was poor, and soreness is definitely up. I don't think anything is injured, but it feels like I pushed the edge.

outbound.subject: Re: Harder session left me cooked
outbound.text:
You pushed close to the edge in yesterday’s 6×3′ session; poor sleep and higher soreness today mean we should deliberately downshift this week and protect recovery.
Today (immediate): keep it very light — optional 10–15 minutes of mobility, easy walking or cycling, foam roll if helpful. No running today unless you feel clearly recovered. Avoid any fast efforts or strides while soreness is up.
This week’s practical plan (keep the two anchors):
- Anchor 1 — one easy aerobic run: 20–30 minutes total, fully aerobic (RPE ~2–3/10). Stay flat, keep cadence light and posture tall, and do NOT add strides. If energy is low, shorten to ~15 minutes and call it done.
- Anchor 2 — one short strength/mobility touch: 20–25 minutes low-load control work (bodyweight, hip/glute activation, calf mobility). Keep it gentle and technical — no heavy loading.
- Saturday long run: easy 25–40 minutes (target 30–40 if you feel recovered). If soreness or fatigue persists, keep the long run to the low end (25–30) or replace with an easy walk/run.
Rules to follow this week: no hard sessions; keep all runs very easy; if soreness or Achilles sharpness increases at any point, stop the session and revert to mobility/rest. If the week tightens further, protect the two anchors only and skip extras.
About strides: postpone the stride test until you have a fresh, well-slept easy run. When you’re ready, follow the controlled test we discussed: 4 × 15 seconds on flat with full recovery (60–90 s easy jog/walk) and stop immediately if Achilles pain increases.
Focus on sleep, hydration, and low-friction routines this week — they will pay the biggest dividends. Let me know how the easy run and Achilles feel after you do it.

inbound.subject: Numbers from the hard session
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 5 out of 10. Risk candidate feels yellow. Intervals yesterday: run 55m total with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Felt exhausted after, soreness high, sleep poor. Pain affects form false. Pain worsening false. No swelling.

outbound.subject: Re: Numbers from the hard session
outbound.text:
Yesterday’s 55‑minute session left you exhausted, with high soreness, poor sleep and a pain score of 5/10 — I’m keeping this week conservative and specific so you recover without losing consistency.
Today (minimum effective dose)
- Do 10–15 minutes of mobility/walk/cycle only. No running unless you feel clearly recovered. Keep it easy and stop if anything sharp or if pain increases.
This week (protected four‑day skeleton, conservative details)
- Easy aerobic run 1: 15–30 minutes, fully aerobic (RPE ~2–3/10). No strides, no pickups. Focus on light cadence and tall posture.
- Easy aerobic run 2 (the easiest session): 15–25 minutes, very easy. Use this as your recovery anchor—stay near the low end of the time range.
- Strength/mobility: 20–25 minutes low‑load control work (bodyweight glute/hip control, single‑leg balance, calf mobility). Keep it gentle; prioritize movement quality over load.
- Saturday long run (deload): reduce planned volume by ~20% from normal. Keep it conservative (target the low end of your usual long run time), fully aerobic and flat.
Safety and pacing rules (non‑negotiable)
- No hard sessions or intensity while your soreness and pain are elevated or risk is yellow. If your energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing completion.
- Postpone strides/fast efforts until you’ve had a fresh, well‑slept easy run with pain clearly reduced.
- Stop immediately for any sharp or increasing Achilles pain; avoid hills and high impact until symptoms settle.
Recovery priorities
- Focus on sleep first, then food and downshifting overall stress — they’re the fastest way back to consistent training.
I’ll keep the plan steady: protect these low‑intensity anchors this week and we’ll reassess next week based on how your soreness, sleep and energy respond. Let me know how the mobility/walk goes and what your soreness and sleep look like tonight.

inbound.subject: Only three days next week
inbound.body: Next week I only have three training days because of work travel again. I'd like the week simplified and I want to protect the most important session, but keep the overall direction the same.

outbound.subject: Re: Only three days next week
outbound.text:
Three training days next week due to travel—let’s keep it ultra-simple and protect the key session while staying true to the plan.
Here’s a three-session layout that preserves the general direction (easy aerobic, low-load strength/mobility, and a very-easy recovery run) without adding volume.
Session 1 — Easy Aerobic: 15–30 minutes, fully aerobic. Keep it relaxed (RPE 2–3/10). No hills or fast pickups. If you’re not recovered or Achilles pain flares, swap to 15–20 minutes of easy walking or cycling and reassess next day.
Session 2 — Low-Load Strength/Mobility: 20–25 minutes. Bodyweight moves only. Focus on gentle, technically tidy work (examples: hip hinges/glute bridges, calf/ankle mobility, light core). No loaded or high-impact work. Stop if any pain increases.
Session 3 — Very-Easy Recovery Run: 15–25 minutes at an easy pace. If energy is low or sleep is poor, substitute with 15–25 minutes of mobility/walk instead. After the run, a brief mobility sequence is fine.
Safety and notes: No hard sessions while soreness or Achilles symptoms are elevated. If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing it. If you rebound tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” missed work. Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and stress management this week.
Long-run note: The long-run anchor remains optional this week; if a window opens, keep it at the low end or skip it this week depending on how you feel.
That’s the plan for next week. Let me know how today’s recovery goes and we’ll adjust as needed.

inbound.subject: Three-day week details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Next week I only have three training days. Week is chaotic false, but volume has to come down. Please simplify the week and protect the key session.

outbound.subject: Re: Three-day week details
outbound.text:
You’ve got the half on 2026-05-17, three training days next week, and Achilles pain at 3/10 — I’ll simplify the week and protect the key session. Here’s the plan to keep progress while staying conservative and responsive to symptoms.
Weekly plan (3 sessions)
1) Session 1 — Easy aerobic (KEY session)
- 20–30 minutes easy run (RPE 2–3, conversational). Keep cadence relaxed, short ground contact, light arm drive. Warm up 5 minutes easy and finish with 2–3 minutes of walking.
- Purpose: maintain aerobic stimulus without stressing the tendon. Protect this session — schedule it on your freshest day.
- If Achilles pain increases (sharp or clearly worse than 3/10), stop and switch to walking or easy cycling.
2) Session 2 — Low-load strength & mobility
- 20–25 minutes: bodyweight control work and mobility. Examples: single-leg balance 2×20–30s, glute bridges 2×10–15, Bulgarian split support (bodyweight) 2×8, light hip hinge, ankle mobility, core brace work.
- Keep loads low, slow tempo, stop any exercise that increases Achilles pain. No heavy/eccentric calf loading.
3) Session 3 — Very-easy aerobic recovery (or low-volume long run if clearly better)
- 15–25 minutes very easy run OR 25–30 minutes only if you wake fresh, pain is reduced, and sleep was good — otherwise keep to the short recovery run or swap to a walk/mobility session.
- Maintain RPE 2–3; no pickups, no strides, no hills.
Execution notes & priorities
- Keep all efforts smooth, relaxed, and technically tidy. The key goal is hit the key session and avoid extra load.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to walking/mobility — do not try to ‘make up’ missed load later in the week.
- Avoid any fast work, pickups, hills, or strides until you have a fresh, well-slept easy run with no symptom increase.
- Stop immediately for any sharp or worsening Achilles pain. If you see swelling or a marked pain jump, pause training and get clinical advice.
Recovery supports
- Prioritize steady sleep, simple meals, and gentle calf/ankle mobility after sessions. Ice briefly if soreness flares after activity and rest the next session if pain remains elevated.
Short summary
- Three controlled sessions: easy aerobic (key), low-load strength, very-easy recovery. Protect the easy aerobic session and respond to pain by switching to walk/cycle or mobility.
Let me know how the first easy aerobic session feels (RPE and any change in Achilles pain) and I’ll adjust next week if needed.

inbound.subject: Race confidence check
inbound.body: With the race getting closer, do you think the current plan still fits where I am? I'm curious what the biggest focus should be over the next two weeks, especially since I've settled into that four-day pattern pretty well.

outbound.subject: Re: Race confidence check
outbound.text:
Yes — the updated conservative base plan still fits. Over the next two weeks we keep one simple priority: protect the easy aerobic consistency while avoiding any intensity that could aggravate your Achilles. You’ve settled into a four-day rhythm, so we’ll use that when available and switch to the ultra-simple 3-day layout for the week you travel.
Key priorities (next 2 weeks)
- Protect the key easy aerobic session — it’s the minimum effective dose for rebuilding fitness.
- Let energy and Achilles pain be the limiter: keep effort smooth and technically tidy, and stop early if either worsens.
- No hills, pickups, strides, or heavy/eccentric calf loading. Focus sleep, food, and stress downshifting.
Two-week plan (clear, actionable)
Week A — standard 4-day rhythm
- Session 1 (key easy aerobic): 20–35 min easy run, RPE 2–3. Keep cadence and form relaxed. Stop and walk/ride if Achilles pain increases from baseline.
- Session 2 (skills): 15–20 min low-load drills and mobility (running drills without fast efforts, A-skips-style marches, single-leg balance, hip mobility). No fast strides.
- Session 3 (strength): 20–25 min low-load control strength (bodyweight single-leg balance, glute bridges, step-ups at low height). Avoid heavy calf loading and eccentric calf work.
- Session 4 (easy aerobic recovery): 15–25 min very easy run. If you’re extra fresh, well-slept, and pain is reduced, this can be a low-volume long run (25–35 min) — otherwise keep it short and easy.
Week B — planned 3-day travel week (ultra-simple)
- 1) Key easy aerobic: 25–30 min easy (freshest day). This is the non-negotiable session.
- 2) Low-load strength/mobility: 20–25 min (bodyweight only).
- 3) Very-easy recovery or optional low-volume long run: 15–30 min depending on how you feel; only extend if Achilles is better and sleep was good.
If–then and safety rules (follow these exactly)
- If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing completion. Short, consistent sessions > long hard ones now.
- If Achilles pain increases (sharp or higher than your current baseline 3/10), stop and swap to walking/cycling and drop intensity for 48–72 hours.
- If you feel better the next day, resume cautiously — do not try to “make up” missed intensity.
- No hard sessions while risk is red-tier.
What I want you to focus on today: take the planned session but remain conservative — smooth effort, short if needed, and prioritize sleep and nutrition tonight.
Let me know how the first easy session goes (RPE and any change in Achilles pain) and I’ll adjust the next week if needed.

inbound.subject: Mild cold and weather
inbound.body: I have a mild cold and the weather is awful this week. Energy is not great. I don't want to do anything dumb, but I also don't want to lose the thread completely. How conservative should I be?

outbound.subject: Re: Mild cold and weather
outbound.text:
Your mild cold and the awful forecast call for caution, so we’ll keep this week conservative and protect the core easy running you’re trying to maintain. You don’t want to overdo it, but you also don’t want to lose the thread. The plan stays simple: guard the key easy aerobic session, avoid any intensity that could aggravate the Achilles, and use very light days when energy is low.
Recommended setup (default 4-day rhythm if energy allows):
- Day 1: Easy aerobic 25–30 minutes at a light conversational pace (RPE 2–3). 5 minutes easy warm-up, 5 minutes cool-down.
- Day 2: Low-load strength/mobility 20–25 minutes (hips, glutes, calves, gentle mobility). No heavy or high-rep work.
- Day 3: Easy aerobic 20–30 minutes (same easy pace) or a very easy recovery day with light mobility if you’re tired.
- Day 4: Very easy recovery 15–25 minutes or, if you’re feeling fresh and sleep has been solid, a short 20–25 minute walk.
If energy or weather restrict you to 3 days:
- Day 1: Easy aerobic 25–30 minutes at an easy pace.
- Day 2: Low-load strength/mobility 20–25 minutes.
- Day 3: Very easy recovery 15–25 minutes or a 15–25 minute easy run only if sleep is good and Achilles pain is not present.
Safety and decision rules you should follow this week:
- No hills, no strides, no tempo, and no heavy calf work. If Achilles pain sharpens or increases, stop immediately and switch to walking or cycling.
- Warm up and cool down gently (3–5 minutes each).
- If sleep drops below about 6 hours or energy stays notably low, trim sessions further or skip a session entirely and rest.
- After each easy run, note your RPE and any change in Achilles symptoms to inform next steps.
This week aligns with the ongoing plan to protect the easy anchor while you recover and wait for better conditions. The next session remains easy aerobic, and we’ll adapt only if pain or sleep metrics indicate the need.
If you want, share how tonight’s sleep was and how you’re feeling in the morning and we’ll fine-tune. Let me know how it goes.
